description: Trace Module 1 — Recorder. Captures macOS user actions (clicks, keystrokes, scrolls, app switches, text input) into a structured trajectory directory that downstream modules (Synthesizer, Runner) consume. Runs as a Python 3.11 FastAPI service on 127.0.0.1:8765 under the /recorder prefix. Uses PyObjC for macOS Accessibility APIs (AXUIElement), Quartz CGEventTap for global event capture, and CGWindowListCreateImage for screenshots. Captures are dual-source: semantic (accessibility tree for clicks/targets) and visual (keyframe screenshots every 5s and on app switches). Text input is aggregated per-field (not per-keystroke) for both usefulness and storage sanity. Events are 1-indexed sequentially, written to events.jsonl as they happen. Output goes to ~/Library/Application Support/Trace/trajectories/<uuid>/ (prod) or Trace-dev/ (Ralph iterations). macOS permissions (Accessibility, Screen Recording, Input Monitoring) required — module detects missing permissions and returns structured errors to the UI. Does NOT interpret the workflow, call any LLM, or write skill files — this is pure capture.

outputStructure.metadata.json: Object with keys: id (uuid), label, started_at (ISO8601), stopped_at (ISO8601 or null), display_info {width, height, scale_factor}, app_focus_history [{bundle_id, name, entered_at, exited_at}]
outputStructure.events.jsonl: One JSON event per line; schema in contracts/trajectory.schema.json
outputStructure.screenshots/NNNN.png: Keyframe screenshots, 4-digit zero-padded sequence number matching an event's seq

eventShape.type: string, one of: click, keypress, scroll, app_switch, window_focus, text_input, keyframe, tap_reenabled
eventShape.target: object or null {role, label, description, frame {x, y, w, h}, ax_identifier}

contracts.coordinateSystem: Screen points (not pixels) — Retina-independent. display_info.scale_factor is stored so consumers can convert if needed.

userStories.title: Package scaffold and uv workspace setup
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the services/recorder Python package scaffolded with uv, pyproject.toml, mypy strict config, ruff config, and pytest config so every later story can run quality checks.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/ exists with src layout (src/recorder/__init__.py, src/recorder/__main__.py)
pyproject.toml declares dependencies: fastapi, uvicorn, pyobjc-framework-Cocoa, pyobjc-framework-Quartz, pyobjc-framework-ApplicationServices, jsonschema, pydantic>=2
dev dependencies: pytest, pytest-asyncio, mypy, ruff, types-* as needed
mypy configured for --strict on recorder package; pyobjc modules ignored via [[tool.mypy.overrides]]
ruff configured with sensible defaults (line length 100, target-version py311)
pytest configured to discover tests in tests/recorder/
uv sync completes without error
uv run ruff check services/recorder passes (no source to lint yet is OK)
uv run mypy --strict services/recorder passes (no source yet is OK)
uv run pytest tests/recorder/ exits 0 with 'no tests collected' — tests directory must exist
userStories.notes: Foundation story. No functional code. Every later Ralph iteration depends on these commands working. Do NOT add application code in this story — keep it purely scaffolding.

userStories.title: JSON schema for trajectory events
userStories.description: As the recorder module, I need a locked JSON schema for trajectory events so downstream modules (Synthesizer, Runner) and tests can validate trajectories structurally.
acceptanceCriteria: contracts/trajectory.schema.json exists at repo root and is a valid JSON Schema draft 2020-12
Schema defines the event shape: seq, timestamp_ms, type, screenshot_ref, app, target, payload
Schema defines type enum: click, keypress, scroll, app_switch, window_focus, text_input, keyframe, tap_reenabled

Schema defines per-type payload shapes (click button+modifiers+drag_to; keypress keys; text_input text+field_label; scroll direction+delta; app_switch from_bundle_id+to_bundle_id; window_focus window_title; keyframe reason)
Schema defines metadata.json shape: id, label, started_at, stopped_at, display_info, app_focus_history
Python helper module services/recorder/src/recorder/schema.py exposes load_event_schema() and validate_event(event_dict) using jsonschema
tests/recorder/test_schema.py: at least 5 valid example events pass validation; at least 5 malformed events fail with useful errors
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the locked contract. Changing it after Synthesizer or Runner have shipped requires coordinated changes. Keep it conservative — err toward required fields being optional if unsure. Adding required fields later is breaking; making optional fields required later is breaking too.

userStories.title: Trajectory writer: directory layout, atomic writes, metadata
userStories.description: As the recorder module, I need a TrajectoryWriter class that creates the trajectory directory, writes metadata.json atomically, appends events to events.jsonl, and writes screenshots with zero-padded filenames, so capture never corrupts an in-progress trajectory.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/writer.py exposes TrajectoryWriter class
Constructor takes a root directory and a label; generates uuid and creates <root>/<uuid>/ and <root>/<uuid>/screenshots/
write_metadata(metadata: dict) writes metadata.json atomically (write to .tmp, fsync, rename)
append_event(event: dict) validates against schema, assigns seq if missing, writes one JSON line to events.jsonl, flushes after write
write_screenshot(seq: int, png_bytes: bytes) writes screenshots/NNNN.png where NNNN is zero-padded 4 digits
close() finalizes stopped_at in metadata and closes the events file handle
Directory permissions set to 0700 on creation
tests/recorder/test_writer.py: round-trip test (write 10 events + 3 screenshots, close, re-read, verify structure)
tests/recorder/test_writer.py: schema-violation test (append_event with bad shape raises ValidationError before writing)
tests/recorder/test_writer.py: concurrent-append test (writer.append_event called from 5 threads produces events with distinct, monotonically increasing seq)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The seq counter must be thread-safe — event tap runs on a background thread and text aggregator runs on the main thread, they both append events.

userStories.title: macOS permission detection and structured errors
userStories.description: As the recorder, I need to detect missing Accessibility, Screen Recording, and Input Monitoring permissions before starting a recording, and return a structured error the UI can act on, so the user isn't left confused by silent failures.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/permissions.py exposes check_permissions() returning a dict {accessibility: bool, screen_recording: bool, input_monitoring: bool}
Accessibility check uses AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions({kAXTrustedCheckOptionPrompt: False}) — must NOT prompt (UI decides when to prompt)
Screen Recording check attempts CGDisplayStreamCreate or CGPreflightScreenCaptureAccess equivalent; returns False if denied
Input Monitoring check is best-effort — attempt to create a listen-only CGEventTap on a no-op event mask and see if it returns a valid tap

A helper get_missing_permissions_error() returns a structured dict: {error: 'missing_permission', permissions: [list of missing], how_to_grant: {permission: human_readable_instructions}} when any are missing, else None
tests/recorder/test_permissions.py: mocks the underlying framework calls and verifies each permission combination produces the right output
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: These checks must not prompt the user — the Tauri UI will own the prompting UX. The check is pure inspection. If PyObjC doesn't expose CGPreflightScreenCaptureAccess directly, use a workaround (attempt to grab a 1x1 screenshot and check for failure).

userStories.title: Event tap: global mouse and keyboard capture
userStories.description: As the recorder, I need a background thread running a passive CGEventTap that captures mouse clicks, key events, and scroll events across all applications, so I can feed them to the event pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/event_tap.py exposes EventTap class with start(), stop(), and a callback registration
Uses CGEventTapCreate with kCGHIDEventTap and kCGEventTapOptionListenOnly — never swallows or modifies events
Event mask covers: mouse down/up (left/right/other), key down, flags changed, scroll wheel
Runs its own CFRunLoop on a dedicated threading.Thread
When OS disables the tap under load (kCGEventTapDisabledByTimeout or kCGEventTapDisabledByUserInput), auto-reenables via CGEventTapEnable and emits a 'tap_reenabled' event to the callback
stop() cleanly exits the run loop and joins the thread with a 2-second timeout
Callback receives a normalized dict: {cg_event_type, timestamp_ms, location_x, location_y, modifiers, key_code (if applicable), scroll_delta (if applicable)}

tests/recorder/test_event_tap.py: starts tap with a mock callback, synthesizes events via CGEventPost, verifies callback receives them (this is a real integration test — mark it with @pytest.mark.macos and skip on CI without a display)
Unit test: tap_reenabled event emitted when CGEventTap is disabled (simulate by posting a fake kCGEventTapDisabledByTimeout)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: This is the most PyObjC-heavy story. Ralph may struggle with PyObjC signatures — if stuck, consult Apple's CGEventTap docs and the pyobjc-framework-Quartz source for exact function bindings. The integration test requires Accessibility permission; document this in tests/recorder/README.md so the human tester can grant it.

userStories.title: Accessibility target resolution at screen coordinates
userStories.description: As the recorder, when a click happens, I need to resolve the AX element under the cursor into a semantic target (role, label, description, frame, ax_identifier) so downstream modules know what was clicked, not just where.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/ax_resolver.py exposes resolve_element_at(x: float, y: float) -> Optional[dict]
Uses AXUIElementCreateSystemWide + AXUIElementCopyElementAtPosition
Returned dict has keys: role, label, description, frame {x, y, w, h}, ax_identifier — any may be None if unavailable, but frame is always present when a target is returned
If the hit element has no label and no description, walks up to ancestors (max 3 levels) looking for a meaningful label — returns the first ancestor with a non-empty label
Timeouts at 200ms total (use a threading.Timer or asyncio.wait_for); returns None on timeout
Handles AXError gracefully — never raises past the module boundary; logs warnings via the standard logging module
tests/recorder/test_ax_resolver.py: integration test against a known macOS app (use Finder — open a window, resolve the toolbar, verify role=AXButton etc.); mark with @pytest.mark.macos
Unit test with mocked AXUIElementCopyElementAtPosition: verifies ancestor walk logic
Unit test: 200ms timeout respected when mock AX calls sleep
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: Many apps have shallow AX trees (especially Electron apps pre-2023). The ancestor walk is critical. If a Ralph iteration gets stuck on PyObjC AX bindings, check ApplicationServices.AXUIElementCopyAttributeValue signatures in the pyobjc source — the return tuple (error_code, value) trips people up.

userStories.title: App and window focus tracking via NSWorkspace notifications
userStories.description: As the recorder, I need to track which app and window is currently focused, emit app_switch events when focus changes, and attach current-app info to every event, so the trajectory reflects cross-app workflows.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/focus_tracker.py exposes FocusTracker class
Subscribes to NSWorkspaceDidActivateApplicationNotification via NSWorkspace.sharedWorkspace().notificationCenter()
Tracks current focused app: {bundle_id, name, pid}
Tracks current focused window title via AXUIElement on the focused app
Exposes get_current_app() -> dict and get_current_window_title() -> Optional[str]
Callback registration: on_app_switch(callback) fires with {from_bundle_id, to_bundle_id, from_name, to_name}; on_window_focus_change(callback) fires with {window_title, app_bundle_id}
Maintains app_focus_history list for metadata.json — appended on every app switch
tests/recorder/test_focus_tracker.py: mocks NSWorkspace notifications, verifies callbacks fire with correct payload
Integration test: starts tracker, switches between two real apps via subprocess (e.g. osascript), verifies events fire; @pytest.mark.macos
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: Window title polling can be expensive — only poll when the focused app changes OR when an AXFocusedWindowChanged notification fires, never on a timer.

userStories.title: Text input aggregation by focused field
userStories.description: As the recorder, I need to aggregate individual keystrokes into per-field text_input events (emitted on field blur or 1.5s idle) rather than storing raw keycode streams, so skills are readable and storage stays manageable.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/text_aggregator.py exposes TextAggregator class
Accepts key events from the event tap (only non-modifier keys — modifier-held combinations are keyboard shortcuts and emit separate keypress events)
Buffers typed characters per (app_bundle_id, ax_identifier-or-frame-fingerprint) field identity
Resolves the current focused text field via AX (AXFocusedUIElement) — if none, falls back to app-level buffering
Emits a text_input event when: (a) focus leaves the field, (b) 1.5 seconds of idle, (c) trajectory stops
text_input event payload: {text: aggregated_string, field_label: ax_label_or_none}
Handles backspace and delete correctly — remove last character from buffer
Does NOT emit text_input events for empty buffers (e.g., user typed 5 chars then deleted all 5)
Pure keyboard shortcuts (anything with cmd/ctrl/opt modifiers held) do NOT enter the text aggregator — they flow through as keypress events
tests/recorder/test_text_aggregator.py: simulates a keystroke sequence, verifies aggregated text_input event is emitted on idle timeout
tests/recorder/test_text_aggregator.py: backspace test (type 'hello', backspace x2, expect 'hel')
tests/recorder/test_text_aggregator.py: field-switch test (type in field A, focus moves to field B, expect field A event emitted before field B buffering begins)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: This is the cleanest semantic gain over pixel-level recording. Sensitive-field redaction is explicitly out of scope for v1 per CLAUDE.md — do NOT add it here even if it seems helpful. For v1, anything typed is captured as-is. That is a deliberate personal-use decision documented in the overview.

userStories.title: Screenshot capture with keyframe policy
userStories.description: As the recorder, I need to capture screenshots as keyframes (not per-event) according to a defined policy — every 5 seconds, on app switch, just before/after each click — so the trajectory has visual context without exploding in size.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/screenshot.py exposes capture_main_display() -> bytes (PNG)
Uses CGWindowListCreateImage with kCGNullWindowID and kCGWindowListOptionOnScreenOnly, kCGWindowImageDefault
Converts NSBitmapImageRep to PNG bytes
services/recorder/src/recorder/keyframe_policy.py exposes KeyframePolicy class
Policy triggers a keyframe on: (a) every 5 seconds while recording, (b) app_switch events, (c) just before each click (pre-click) and just after (post-click, 100ms delay)
Exposes should_capture(event_type, seconds_since_last_keyframe) -> bool
Keyframe events in events.jsonl have type='keyframe' with payload {reason: 'periodic'|'app_switch'|'pre_click'|'post_click'}
Screenshots are referenced by the event's seq, filename pattern screenshots/NNNN.png
tests/recorder/test_keyframe_policy.py: unit tests for each trigger condition
tests/recorder/test_screenshot.py: integration test that captures a real screenshot and verifies PNG validity (file starts with PNG magic bytes, Pillow can open it); @pytest.mark.macos
Retina sanity check: captured screenshot dimensions match display_info resolution
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: On Retina displays, CGWindowListCreateImage returns pixel-density images. metadata.json's display_info.scale_factor must reflect this so the Runner can map click coordinates correctly. Test this on a real Retina Mac — do not trust unit tests here.

userStories.title: Recording session orchestrator wiring all capture components together
userStories.description: As the recorder, I need a RecordingSession class that starts/stops all capture components (event tap, focus tracker, text aggregator, keyframe timer), multiplexes their events into the trajectory writer with monotonic seq, and handles clean shutdown, so recording works as a unified whole.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/session.py exposes RecordingSession class

start(label: str) -> session_id: initializes TrajectoryWriter, checks permissions (aborts with structured error if missing), starts EventTap, FocusTracker, TextAggregator, and a periodic keyframe timer
stop() -> summary: stops all components, flushes in-flight text_input buffers, closes the writer, returns {trajectory_id, event_count, duration_ms}
Registered callbacks convert raw capture-component events into schema-valid events and append them via TrajectoryWriter
A single threading.Lock guards seq assignment and writer.append_event calls
Click events trigger: pre-click keyframe → AX target resolution → click event with target → post-click keyframe (100ms delayed)
Idempotent stop() — calling stop twice is safe
Max one active session at a time — calling start() while active raises a clear error
tests/recorder/test_session.py: mock out EventTap and FocusTracker, feed synthetic events, verify trajectory directory structure and event sequence
Integration test: start a session, do nothing for 10 seconds, stop — expect at least 2 periodic keyframes, one app_switch (or none), metadata with correct timestamps; @pytest.mark.macos
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the integration point where the event-tap thread hands events to the writer. The lock around seq assignment + writer.append_event is critical — without it, seq values can collide under high event rate.

userStories.title: FastAPI service mounting recorder endpoints on the gateway
userStories.description: As the Tauri frontend, I need HTTP endpoints to start/stop recording, check recording status, list trajectories, fetch a specific trajectory, and delete one, so the UI can drive the recorder.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/api.py exposes a FastAPI APIRouter mounted at /recorder in the gateway
POST /recorder/start body {label?: string} — starts a session, returns {trajectory_id} or 409 if already recording, or 403 with permissions error if any missing
POST /recorder/stop no body — stops the active session, returns {trajectory_id, event_count, duration_ms} or 409 if not recording
GET /recorder/status — returns {recording: bool, current_trajectory_id: string|null, started_at: string|null}
GET /trajectories — lists all trajectories with {id, label, started_at, duration_ms, event_count}
GET /trajectories/{id} — returns full metadata + events + screenshot URLs (served as static files)
DELETE /trajectories/{id} — removes the trajectory directory; 404 if not found; 409 if it is the active one
Static file mount at /trajectories/{id}/screenshots/{filename} serves PNGs from the trajectory directory
gateway/main.py creates the FastAPI app and mounts the recorder router
tests/recorder/test_api.py uses httpx.AsyncClient against the FastAPI app: tests each endpoint with mocked RecordingSession
tests/recorder/test_api.py: start-while-recording returns 409
tests/recorder/test_api.py: start-without-permissions returns 403 with structured error body
uv run uvicorn gateway.main:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8765 starts cleanly
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The gateway will later also mount /synthesize and /run — keep gateway/main.py structured so other modules can add their routers without touching recorder code. Bind to 127.0.0.1 ONLY — never 0.0.0.0 — this service must not be network-reachable.

userStories.title: Trajectory index SQLite for fast list queries
userStories.description: As the API, I need a SQLite index of trajectories so GET /trajectories is fast even with hundreds of recordings and doesn't have to scan every directory on disk.
acceptanceCriteria: services/recorder/src/recorder/index_db.py exposes IndexDB class backed by SQLite at <app_support>/index.db
Schema: trajectories(id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, label TEXT, started_at TEXT, stopped_at TEXT, event_count INTEGER, duration_ms INTEGER)
TrajectoryWriter inserts a row on creation and updates it on close()
DELETE /trajectories/{id} removes the row
GET /trajectories reads from the index, not the filesystem
A reconcile() method scans the trajectories directory and rebuilds the index (for recovery from a crash or manual filesystem edit)
Startup of the recorder service runs reconcile() once
tests/recorder/test_index_db.py: insert, update, delete, list, reconcile
tests/recorder/test_index_db.py: reconcile test — delete a trajectory directory by hand while its row exists, run reconcile, verify row removed
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: SQLite is enough for v1 — do not introduce an ORM. Use sqlite3 directly with parameterized queries.

userStories.title: Reference trajectory fixtures for the five reference workflows
userStories.description: As downstream modules (Synthesizer, Runner) and as an acceptance bar for the recorder itself, I need canonical recorded trajectories for each of the five reference workflows committed to the repo so downstream tests can run without re-recording.
acceptanceCriteria: fixtures/trajectories/gmail_reply/ exists with metadata.json, events.jsonl, and a screenshots/ subdirectory (screenshots may be small synthetic PNGs to keep repo size down, as long as they are valid PNGs)
Same for calendar_block, finder_organize, slack_status, notes_daily
Each fixture passes validation via schema.validate_event on every line of events.jsonl
Each fixture has at least one click with a resolved AX target, one text_input with a field_label, and one app_switch
A script scripts/regenerate_fixtures.sh documents how a human can re-record these on a real machine (not run by Ralph — human-only)
tests/recorder/test_fixtures.py: iterates all 5 fixtures, parses them, asserts per-fixture minimums above
tests/recorder/test_fixtures.py: passes
A tests/recorder/README.md section documents the fixtures and how to regenerate them
Total fixtures directory size under 5MB (compress screenshots if needed)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: For the Ralph loop, you can generate these fixtures synthetically (hand-crafted JSON that looks like what a real recording would produce). A human will replace them with real recordings during smoke testing. This is the one story where Ralph is NOT expected to produce real user data — synthetic fixtures that pass the schema are acceptable.

userStories.title: End-to-end integration test of the recorder via the HTTP API
userStories.description: As a confidence check, I need an integration test that drives the recorder end-to-end via its HTTP API — starts a recording, simulates a short synthetic workflow, stops, and validates the resulting trajectory — so regressions are caught.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/recorder/test_e2e.py starts the FastAPI app via httpx ASGI transport
Test mocks EventTap, FocusTracker, and screenshot capture (all macOS-dependent pieces) with fakes that emit deterministic synthetic events
Test: POST /recorder/start → expect {trajectory_id}
Test: emit 5 fake clicks, 2 fake text inputs, 1 fake app switch via the fakes
Test: POST /recorder/stop → expect {trajectory_id, event_count >= 8}
Test: GET /trajectories/{id} → response validates against expected structure
Test: DELETE /trajectories/{id} → 200, then GET /trajectories/{id} returns 404
Test passes end-to-end without requiring macOS permissions (because all macOS pieces are mocked)
pytest passes
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
userStories.notes: The point of mocking the macOS pieces here is so this test runs in any environment — Ralph's sandbox, CI, whatever. Real macOS verification happens in the human smoke test (R-015).

userStories.title: Human smoke test checklist for real-machine verification
userStories.description: As the final acceptance gate, I need a smoke test checklist that a human runs on a real Mac after the Ralph loop completes, covering all 5 reference workflows recorded live, so we know the recorder actually works on real hardware.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/recorder/smoke.md exists as a human-checklist markdown file

Checklist section per reference workflow with checkboxes: [ ] permissions granted, [ ] started recording via POST /recorder/start, [ ] performed the workflow on a real machine, [ ] stopped recording, [ ] GET /trajectories/{id} returns sensible data

Per-workflow expected signals: event counts in a reasonable range, at least N clicks with resolved AX targets (N listed per workflow), at least one text_input event, at least one app_switch, at least 5 keyframe screenshots
Storage sanity: trajectory directory size is under 30MB for a 3-minute recording
Kill-tap test: checklist item to hog CPU for 10 seconds during recording, verify a tap_reenabled event appears in events.jsonl afterwards
Permission denial test: revoke Accessibility permission, POST /recorder/start, verify 403 response with structured error
The smoke.md file includes a 'sign-off' section at the end where the human tester writes the date and the macOS version they tested on
The Ralph loop MUST NOT output the completion promise until the smoke.md has the sign-off section filled in — Ralph should detect this and report 'awaiting human smoke test' instead
userStories.notes: This is the story that prevents Ralph from declaring victory on pure unit-test pass. A real human has to run the workflows on a real Mac. The Ralph loop must refuse to output RECORDER_DONE if the sign-off is missing — implement this as a pre-promise check in the prompt.md / CLAUDE.md instructions.

scope: Main display only (no multi-monitor awareness in v1)
No password/sensitive-field redaction in v1 (documented personal-use decision)
No shell execution by the recorder (it is pure capture)

performance.eventLatency: < 50ms from user action to event appended to events.jsonl
performance.axResolution: < 200ms per click (hard timeout, returns null on timeout)
performance.storage: < 30MB per 3 minutes of typical workflow recording

resources.apiCost: $0 — this module does NOT call any LLM

macOSPermissions: Accessibility (required for event tap and AX queries)
Input Monitoring (required for CGEventTap on some macOS versions)

security.network: FastAPI binds to 127.0.0.1 ONLY. Never 0.0.0.0. Not network-reachable.
security.filesystem: All trajectory data under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/ with dir perms 0700. No writes outside this tree.
security.secrets: This module does not handle any secrets or API keys. No Anthropic calls.
security.dataHandling: Plaintext on disk. No encryption at rest in v1. Documented personal-use decision — revisit before any multi-user scenarios.
ralphGuardrails: Ralph iterations write to Trace-dev/, never Trace/

Ralph iterations do NOT have Accessibility or Screen Recording permissions in their sandbox — integration tests using real macOS APIs must be marked @pytest.mark.macos and skip gracefully when permissions are missing
Ralph MUST NOT output the completion promise until tests/recorder/smoke.md has a filled-in human sign-off section

nonGoals: LLM-based interpretation of the trajectory (that is the Synthesizer's job)
A UI — this module is a pure HTTP service; the Tauri frontend is a separate branch
Drag-and-drop capture fidelity (v1 represents drags as click pairs with acknowledged imperfection)
Recording during login screens or secure fields handled by the system

successMetrics.schemaStability: No breaking changes to contracts/trajectory.schema.json after R-002 is merged
successMetrics.referenceWorkflowCoverage: All 5 reference workflows record with a trajectory that passes R-015 smoke criteria
successMetrics.testCoverage: > 85% line coverage on services/recorder/ via pytest-cov (excluding PyObjC-wrapper modules which require real macOS APIs)
successMetrics.cleanQualityGates: uv run ruff check, uv run mypy --strict, uv run pytest all pass at every commit

openQuestions: Drag-and-drop: v1 captures as click-pairs. Is that fidelity enough for the reference workflows (finder_organize has drag intent)? Revisit after R-015.
App-focus polling interval: we rely on NSWorkspace notifications but some apps don't fire them cleanly (older Electron). Is a 2-second poll fallback needed? Defer decision to smoke test results.
events.jsonl fsync frequency: flush-after-write is probably enough, but under a hard crash we may lose a few events. Acceptable for v1 — flag for v2.
Where does the recorder service run? For v1 it's spawned by the Tauri app as a subprocess. Document the process supervision approach once integration branch starts.
